one_line_takeaway: DeRa lets you dial an aligned LM's KL-regularization strength at decoding time by linearly blending reference and aligned logits (softmax[lambda*h_aligned + (1-lambda)*h_sft]), faithfully mimicking retrained models and cheaply locating better regularization strengths without retraining.
key_finding: DeRa closely tracks models retrained from scratch at the same effective KL strength beta/lambda, and this agreement holds even in the extrapolation regime lambda>1 where the combined logits lie outside the convex hull of the reference and aligned logits. Consequently DeRa can identify KL strengths that beat the default beta: in the TL;DR task it revealed the base aligned model was over-regularized, and reducing regularization (lambda>1) raised the win rate against the reference, a gain confirmed by retrained models; it can likewise flag under-regularized models (lambda<1). The toy task confirms fidelity (matching length reward and length distributions between DeRa and retrained), while also surfacing reward hacking (models exploit the length reward by truncating copied text) that is mitigated by choosing a large enough regularization strength.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Decoding-time Realignment (DeRa), a method to adjust the KL-regularization strength of an RLHF/DPO-aligned language model at inference time without retraining. Its theoretical core is a proof (from the closed-form solution of the KL-regularized reward objective) that all aligned models sharing the same reward but differing in KL strength are geometric mixtures of a single reference (SFT) model and a single aligned model, differing only in their mixing weight. DeRa is an autoregressive, per-token approximation of these geometric mixtures. Proposition 1 shows the realigned next-token distribution reduces to softmax[ lambda*h_aligned(beta) + (1-lambda)*h_sft ], i.e. a simple linear interpolation of the reference and aligned logits. The decoding-time parameter lambda maps the effective KL strength to beta/lambda: lambda=0 recovers the reference/SFT model, lambda=1 recovers the base aligned model, and lambda>1 (e.g. 2, 5, 10) extrapolates to weaker regularization than the model was trained with. This lets practitioners sweep regularization strengths, tune per-user or per-task, and cheaply localize a promising beta before (optionally) retraining.
threat_model: N/A. This is a machine-learning methods paper on alignment/decoding, not a security paper. It has no adversary, no attacker capabilities, and no defended asset. Alignment appears only in the standard RLHF sense of reducing errors, biases, and harmful outputs (e.g. a 'how to make a fake credit card' refusal example used to illustrate tone control); the paper does not model an attacker attempting to subvert the model, and no threat model is claimed or should be inferred.
limitations: Stated by the authors: combining logits from two models doubles decoding time and memory versus standard decoding; a cheaper weight-combining alternative (Appendix D.5) works but incurs a performance penalty consistent with prior weight-merging literature. The method is an autoregressive per-token approximation of a sequence-level geometric mixture, so it is not exact and is not expected to perfectly match the retrained model (the paper only claims strong correlation). It requires simultaneous access to both the reference/SFT and aligned model logits. Extrapolating lambda too high reintvroduces reward hacking and loss of coherence/fluency (verbatim copying in the hallucination task, degraded text at high lambda in the credit-card example). Beyond authors' own notes: much of the evaluation is qualitative or relies on an LLM-as-judge (PaLM 2) and figures rather than reported numeric metrics, so effect sizes are hard to read off precisely; the largest models tested are 7B-class (Zephyr) or proprietary PaLM 2, without broad multi-benchmark quantitative tables.

datasets_used: XSum (SFT for the toy length-reward summarization task, T5-small)
Reddit TL;DR summarization dataset (Stiennon et al. 2020; SFT, reward-model training, and evaluation over 1000 test-fold queries)
UltraFeedback (Cui et al. 2023; binary preference data used to DPO-align Zephyr-7b)
MT-Bench (Zheng et al. 2023; used to evaluate alignment level of realigned Zephyr-7b across lambda)
A pro/con-argument rewriting dataset for the RAG hallucination-mitigation task (details in Appendix D.4)
quantitative_results: Base training-time KL regularization strength beta = 0.1 in all experiments
TL;DR: lambda swept over {0.5, 2/3, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0}, corresponding to effective KL strengths beta/lambda of {0.2, 0.15, 0.1, 0.05, 0.02, 0.01}
Hallucination task: lambda swept over {0.011, 0.1, 0.5, 0.67, 1, 2, 5, 10}, corresponding to beta/lambda of {9, 1, 0.2, 0.15, 0.1, 0.05, 0.02, 0.01}
Zephyr-7b MT-Bench eval: lambda varied from 0 to 2.0 in increments of 0.2

Win rates judged by PaLM 2 Large (text-unicorn-001) over 1000 test queries; DeRa win-rate curves lie above the base-beta red line for lambda>1, and DeRa vs retrained win rates stay near parity even for lambda in {2,5,10} (results shown as Figure 3 curves, not a numeric table)
lambda=0 recovers the reference/SFT model; lambda=1 recovers the base aligned model; lambda>1 corresponds to smaller (weaker) regularization beta/lambda than the base beta
Length-reward toy task: reward increases monotonically with lambda for both DeRa and retrained, with matching response-length distributions (rewarded length range [40,50])
baselines_compared: Models retrained from scratch at the target KL strength beta/lambda (the primary comparison; DeRa is validated as an approximation to these)
The reference / SFT model (pi_sft), i.e. lambda=0
The base aligned model pi_theta(beta), i.e. lambda=1

Conceptually contrasted (not empirically benchmarked) against related merging/decoding methods: Emulated Fine-Tuning / proxy-tuning (Mitchell et al. 2024; Liu et al. 2024a), rewarded soups / weight-interpolation and model-ensemble multi-reward methods (Rame et al. 2023; Jang et al. 2023), and logit-merging decoders (fusion, contrastive decoding, classifier-free guidance, speculative decoding)
